content:
# Exodus Chapter 16 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~42v-43r (PDF pp. 79-80); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 57-58 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_016_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_016_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_016_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_016_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- PROSE NARRATIVE WITH EMBEDDED DIVINE SPEECH:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 16 in standard prose column format with no special scribal layout. This chapter -- one of the longest in Exodus -- contains extensive divine speech (vv. 4-5, 8, 12, 16-18, 23, 25-26, 28-29, 32-33), the first Torah legislation regarding the Sabbath (vv. 23-30), and the only instance of the phrase *man hu* ("What is it?") as an etymology for manna (v. 15). A *petuchah* appears before 16:1, marking the transition from the Elim oasis to the wilderness of Sin. A *setumah* before v. 4 separates the people's murmuring from YHWH's response. Further *setumah* markers appear before vv. 6, 9, 11, 13, 16, 19, and 28. A *petuchah* before v. 32 marks the shift from immediate provision to memorial institution. A *setumah* before v. 35 introduces the summary note about the forty years of manna.

---

## The Murmuring of the Congregation (vv. 1-3)

**16:1** And they journeyed from Elim, and all the congregation of the sons of Israel came to the wilderness of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after their going out from the land of Egypt. [fn1]

**16:2** And all the congregation of the sons of Israel grumbled against Moses and against Aaron in the wilderness. [fn2]

**16:3** And the sons of Israel said to them, "Would that we had died by the hand of YHWH in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the pot of meat, when we ate bread to the full -- for you have brought us out into this wilderness to kill all this assembly with hunger." [fn3]

---

## YHWH Promises Bread from Heaven (vv. 4-8)

**16:4** And YHWH said to Moses, "Behold, I am about to rain bread from heaven for you. And the people shall go out and gather the portion of a day in its day, so that I may test them -- whether they will walk in My instruction or not. [fn4]

**16:5** And it shall be on the sixth day, that they shall prepare what they bring in, and it shall be double over what they gather day by day." [fn5]

**16:6** And Moses and Aaron said to all the sons of Israel, "At evening you shall know that YHWH brought you out from the land of Egypt, [fn6]

**16:7** and in the morning you shall see the glory of YHWH, in that He hears your grumblings against YHWH. And what are we, that you grumble against us?" [fn7]

**16:8** And Moses said, "When YHWH gives you in the evening meat to eat, and bread in the morning to the full -- in that YHWH hears your grumblings which you grumble against Him -- and what are we? Your grumblings are not against us, but against YHWH." [fn8]

---

## The Glory of YHWH Appears (vv. 9-12)

**16:9** And Moses said to Aaron, "Say to all the congregation of the sons of Israel, 'Draw near before YHWH, for He has heard your grumblings.'" [fn9]

**16:10** And it came to pass, as Aaron spoke to all the congregation of the sons of Israel, that they turned toward the wilderness, and behold, the glory of YHWH appeared in the cloud. [fn10]

**16:11** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:

**16:12** "I have heard the grumblings of the sons of Israel. Speak to them, saying, 'Between the evenings you shall eat meat, and in the morning you shall be filled with bread -- and you shall know that I am YHWH your God.'" [fn11]

---

## Quail and Manna -- "What Is It?" (vv. 13-15)

**16:13** And it came to pass in the evening that the quail came up and covered the camp. And in the morning there was a layer of dew around the camp. [fn12]

**16:14** And the layer of dew went up, and behold, upon the face of the wilderness -- a fine, flaky thing, fine as frost upon the ground. [fn13]

**16:15** And the sons of Israel saw it, and they said each man to his brother, "*Man hu!*" -- "What is it?" -- for they did not know what it was. And Moses said to them, "It is the bread which YHWH has given you to eat." [fn14]

---

## Instructions for Gathering (vv. 16-21)

**16:16** "This is the thing which YHWH has commanded: 'Gather of it, each man according to his eating -- an omer per head, according to the number of your persons. Each man for those who are in his tent shall you take.'" [fn15]

**16:17** And the sons of Israel did so, and they gathered -- some more and some less.

**16:18** And they measured with the omer, and the one who gathered much had nothing over, and the one who gathered little had no lack. Each man according to his eating they gathered. [fn16]

**16:19** And Moses said to them, "Let no man leave any of it until morning." [fn17]

**16:20** And they did not listen to Moses, and some men left some of it until morning, and it bred worms and stank. And Moses was angry with them. [fn18]

**16:21** And they gathered it morning by morning, each man according to his eating. And when the sun grew hot, it melted. [fn19]

---

## The Sabbath -- A Solemn Rest (vv. 22-30)

**16:22** And it came to pass on the sixth day, they gathered double bread -- two omers for each one. And all the leaders of the congregation came and told Moses. [fn20]

**16:23** And he said to them, "This is what YHWH has spoken: A solemn rest, a holy Sabbath to YHWH, is tomorrow. What you would bake, bake, and what you would boil, boil. And all that is left over, set aside for yourselves to keep until the morning." [fn21]

**16:24** And they set it aside until the morning, as Moses had commanded. And it did not stink, and no worm was in it. [fn22]

**16:25** And Moses said, "Eat it today, for today is a Sabbath to YHWH. Today you will not find it in the field. [fn23]

**16:26** Six days you shall gather it, and on the seventh day -- the Sabbath -- there shall be none."

**16:27** And it came to pass on the seventh day that some of the people went out to gather, and they did not find. [fn24]

**16:28** And YHWH said to Moses, "How long do you refuse to keep My commandments and My instructions?" [fn25]

**16:29** "See that YHWH has given you the Sabbath. Therefore He gives you on the sixth day bread of two days. Remain, each man in his place; let no man go out from his place on the seventh day." [fn26]

**16:30** And the people rested on the seventh day. [fn27]

---

## The Manna Described and the Memorial Jar (vv. 31-36)

**16:31** And the house of Israel called its name *man*. And it was like the seed of coriander, white, and its taste was like a wafer with honey. [fn28]

**16:32** And Moses said, "This is the thing which YHWH has commanded: 'A full omer of it is to be kept for your generations, so that they may see the bread with which I fed you in the wilderness, when I brought you out from the land of Egypt.'" [fn29]

**16:33** And Moses said to Aaron, "Take a jar and put in it a full omer of manna, and set it before YHWH, to be kept for your generations." [fn30]

**16:34** As YHWH commanded Moses, so Aaron set it down before the Testimony, to be kept. [fn31]

**16:35** And the sons of Israel ate the manna forty years, until they came to an inhabited land. The manna they ate until they came to the border of the land of Canaan. [fn32]

**16:36** (And the omer is a tenth of the ephah.) [fn33]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Vayyis'u me-Elim*: "And they journeyed from Elim" -- the wilderness of Sin and the precise chronology** -- Hebrew: *vayyis'u me-Elim vayyavo'u kol-adat benei-Yisra'el el-midbar-Sin asher bein-Elim u-vein Sinai ba-chamishah asar yom la-chodesh ha-sheni le-tsetam me-erets Mitsrayim*. The noun *adat* (construct of *edah*, "congregation, assembly," from *ya'ad*, "to appoint, meet by appointment") denotes the formal gathered community. The place *midbar-Sin* ("wilderness of Sin") is not to be confused with *Sinai*; the name *Sin* is of uncertain etymology, possibly related to the Akkadian moon-god. The precise date -- the fifteenth day of the second month -- places this event exactly one month after the exodus (departure from Egypt on the fifteenth of the first month, cf. Num. 33:3). EMET renders "the midbar [wilderness] of Sin" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "the wilderness of Sin." PNEUMA renders "the wilderness of Sin." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA -- "wilderness" in English without transliteration. LXX: *eis tēn erēmon Sin* ("into the wilderness of Sin").

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Vayyillonu*: "grumbled" -- the escalation of complaint** -- Hebrew: *vayyillonu kol-adat benei-Yisra'el al-Mosheh ve-al-Aharon ba-midbar*. The Niphal of *lun* ("to murmur, grumble") recurs from 15:24, but the complaint has escalated: at Marah it was "the people" (*ha-am*); here it is "all the congregation of the sons of Israel" -- the entire formal assembly. And the complaint is now directed against both Moses and Aaron, not Moses alone. EMET renders "grumbled" (*vayillinu*). LOGOS renders "murmured." PNEUMA renders "complained bitterly." The consensus renders "grumbled" -- following EMET. "Grumbled" was established as the consensus rendering for *lun* in EXO 15:24 and is maintained for consistency. "Murmured" (LOGOS) is slightly archaic; "complained bitterly" (PNEUMA) adds an intensifier not present in the Hebrew. LXX: *diegonggusen* ("murmured throughout").

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Mi-yitten mutenu*: "Would that we had died" -- the death-wish formula and the nostalgia of slavery** -- Hebrew: *vayyomeru aleihem benei Yisra'el mi-yitten mutenu be-yad-YHWH be-erets Mitsrayim be-shivtenu al-sir ha-basar be-okhlenu lechem la-sova ki-hotsatem otanu el-ha-midbar ha-zeh le-hamit et-kol-ha-qahal ha-zeh ba-ra'av*. The expression *mi-yitten* ("who will give?" = "would that! / if only!") followed by the infinitive construct *mutenu* ("our dying") is the standard Hebrew optative for an impossible wish. This is the first occurrence of the "death-wish" motif that recurs throughout the wilderness narrative (cf. Num. 11:15; 14:2; 20:3). The romanticized memory of Egypt is striking: *sir ha-basar* ("the pot of meat," from *sir*, "cooking pot" + *basar*, "flesh, meat") and *lechem la-sova* ("bread to the full") idealize the food of slavery. The word *qahal* ("assembly, congregation") is a near-synonym of *edah* but emphasizes the gathered crowd. The accusation *le-hamit...ba-ra'av* ("to kill...with hunger") charges Moses and Aaron with deliberate mass murder by starvation. EMET renders "Would that [mi yitten] we had died [muteinu]" with extensive transliteration. LOGOS renders "Would that we had died." PNEUMA renders "If only YHWH had let us die in Egypt." All three preserve the optative force. The consensus renders "Would that we had died" -- following EMET/LOGOS for the formal optative. PNEUMA's "If only" is a valid gloss but introduces "YHWH had let us die," which restructures the Hebrew syntax; the MT reads *mi-yitten mutenu be-yad YHWH* ("would that we had died by the hand of YHWH"), not "would that YHWH had killed us." The phrase "by the hand of YHWH" is retained. For *sir ha-basar*, the consensus renders "the pot of meat" -- following LOGOS. EMET renders "the pot [sir] of flesh [ha-basar]" with transliteration; "meat" is the contextually appropriate English rendering for *basar* in a food context (cf. "flesh" for bodily contexts). LXX: *ophelen apethanomen plēgentes hupo kuriou* ("would that we had died, struck by the Lord"), adding *plēgentes* ("struck") not in the MT.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Hineni mamtir lakhem lechem min-ha-shamayim*: "Behold, I am about to rain bread from heaven for you" -- the *lechem min-ha-shamayim* and the torah test** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh hineni mamtir lakhem lechem min-ha-shamayim ve-yatsa ha-am ve-laqetu devar-yom be-yomo lema'an anassenu ha-yelekh be-torati im-lo*. The Hiphil participle *mamtir* ("causing to rain," from *matar*) with *hineni* ("behold me") presents YHWH Himself as the source of the heavenly rain. The phrase *lechem min-ha-shamayim* ("bread from heaven") becomes one of the defining images of the wilderness period (cf. Neh. 9:15; Ps. 78:24; John 6:31-32). The clause *devar-yom be-yomo* ("the portion of a day in its day") establishes the principle of daily dependence -- no hoarding, no surplus. The Piel *anassenu* ("I may test them," from *nasah*) reveals that the manna is simultaneously provision and test: *ha-yelekh be-torati im-lo* ("whether they will walk in My instruction or not"). The noun *torah* here means "instruction" in its broadest sense -- pre-Sinai but already operative. EMET renders "Behold me [hineni] raining [mamtir]...bread from the shamayim [heavens]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "Behold, I am about to rain bread from heaven." PNEUMA renders "I am going to rain down bread from heaven." The consensus renders "Behold, I am about to rain bread from heaven" -- following LOGOS. EMET's transliterated *shamayim* is replaced with "heaven" for readability; the "Behold" preserves the Hebrew *hineni* construction. PNEUMA's "rain down" adds a directional particle ("down") not in the Hebrew. For *devar-yom be-yomo*, the consensus renders "the portion of a day in its day" -- following LOGOS's "the matter of a day in its day" but substituting "portion" for "matter," as the context is clearly about daily rations rather than abstract "matters." For *be-torati*, the consensus renders "My instruction" -- following LOGOS. LXX: *idou egō huō humin artous ek tou ouranou* ("behold, I rain for you bread from heaven").

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Mishneh*: "double" -- the sixth-day preparation** -- Hebrew: *ve-hayah ba-yom ha-shishi ve-hekhinu et asher-yavi'u ve-hayah mishneh al asher-yilqetu yom yom*. The Hiphil *ve-hekhinu* (from *kun*, "to establish, prepare") means "they shall prepare." The noun *mishneh* ("double, second portion," from *shanah*, "to repeat") anticipates the Sabbath legislation in vv. 22-30. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *diploun* ("double") for *mishneh*.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Erev vi-da'tem*: "At evening you shall know" -- experiential knowledge through provision** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer Mosheh ve-Aharon el-kol-benei Yisra'el erev vi-da'tem ki YHWH hotsi etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim*. The knowledge (*yada*, "to know") will be experiential -- they will know YHWH's deliverance through the tangible arrival of food at evening. EMET renders "[At] evening [erev]." LOGOS renders "At evening." PNEUMA renders "By evening." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "At evening." LXX: *hesperas gnōsesthe* ("in the evening you shall know").

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Kevod YHWH*: "the glory of YHWH" -- the divine *kavod* manifest** -- Hebrew: *u-voqer u-re'item et-kevod YHWH be-shom'o et-telunnoteikhem al-YHWH ve-nakhnu mah ki tallinu aleinu*. The noun *kavod* ("glory, weight, heaviness, honor," from *kaved*, "to be heavy, weighty") denotes the manifest, visible, weighty presence of YHWH. In the morning they will see (*ra'ah*) this glory -- the manna itself is a manifestation of divine *kavod*. The rhetorical question *ve-nakhnu mah* ("and what are we?") deflects: the grumbling is not against Moses and Aaron but against YHWH. All three translators render "the glory of YHWH/the LORD." The consensus renders "the glory of YHWH" -- with YHWH transliterated per convention. For *telunnoteikhem*, the consensus renders "grumblings" -- maintaining consistency with the *lun* word-group established in fn2. LOGOS renders "murmurings"; PNEUMA renders "complaints." LXX: *tēn doxan kuriou* ("the glory of the Lord").

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Be-tet YHWH lakhem*: "When YHWH gives you" -- the structure of provision** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer Mosheh be-tet YHWH lakhem ba-erev basar le-ekhol ve-lechem ba-boqer lisbo'a bi-shmo'a YHWH et-telunnoteikhem asher-atem mallinim alav ve-nakhnu mah lo aleinu telunnoteikhem ki al-YHWH*. The infinitive construct *be-tet* ("in the giving of," from *natan*) with *YHWH* as subject establishes the evening-meat / morning-bread structure corresponding to quail at dusk and manna at dawn. The Hiphil participle *mallinim* ("ones grumbling") from *lun* insists that the grumbling is directed at YHWH, not at human leaders. EMET renders "in YHWH's giving [be-tet]." LOGOS renders "When the LORD gives." PNEUMA renders "YHWH will give you." The consensus renders "When YHWH gives" -- following LOGOS for English clarity with YHWH transliterated. LXX: *en tō didonai kurion humin* ("in the Lord's giving you").

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Qirvu lifnei YHWH*: "Draw near before YHWH"** -- Hebrew: *emor el-kol-adat benei-Yisra'el qirvu lifnei YHWH ki shama et telunnoteikhem*. The Qal imperative *qirvu* (from *qarav*, "to draw near, approach") commands approach to YHWH's presence -- the root of *qorban* ("offering, sacrifice"). All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS, with "Draw near" for *qirvu*. PNEUMA's "Come into the presence" loses the specific verb. LXX: *prosélthate enantion tou theou* ("come near before God").

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Kevod YHWH nir'ah be-anan*: "the glory of YHWH appeared in the cloud" -- the theophanic *kavod*** -- Hebrew: *vayehi ke-dabber Aharon el-kol-adat benei-Yisra'el vayifnu el-ha-midbar ve-hinneh kevod YHWH nir'ah be-anan*. The Niphal *nir'ah* (from *ra'ah*, "to see") means "appeared, was seen, became visible." The *anan* ("cloud") is the theophanic cloud that led Israel from Egypt (13:21-22). This is the first explicit appearance of the *kevod YHWH* in Exodus (it will become central in chs. 24, 33-34, 40). All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *hē doxa kuriou ōphthē en nephelē* ("the glory of the Lord appeared in a cloud").

> **[fn11] v.12 -- *Bein ha-arbayim*: "Between the evenings" -- the twilight formula** -- Hebrew: *shamati et-telunnot benei Yisra'el dabber aleihem lemor bein ha-arbayim tokhlu vasar u-va-boqer tisbe'u-lachem vi-da'tem ki ani YHWH Eloheikhem*. The phrase *bein ha-arbayim* ("between the evenings," literally "between the two evenings") is a technical expression for the twilight period (also used for the Passover sacrifice, 12:6). The dual *arbayim* is debated: either between sunset and full darkness, or between the sun's decline and sunset. The recognition formula *vi-da'tem ki ani YHWH* ("and you shall know that I am YHWH") is fundamental to Exodus theology (cf. 6:7; 7:5, 17). EMET renders "Between the evenings [bein ha-arbayim]." LOGOS renders "Between the evenings." PNEUMA renders "At twilight." The consensus renders "Between the evenings" -- following EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew dual form is distinctive and theologically resonant; PNEUMA's "At twilight," while conveying the sense, loses the characteristic Hebrew idiom. For *telunnot*, the consensus renders "grumblings" -- maintaining consistency. LXX: *to pros hesperan* ("toward evening") for *bein ha-arbayim*.

> **[fn12] v.13 -- *Ha-selav*: "the quail" -- migratory provision** -- Hebrew: *vayehi va-erev vatta'al ha-selav vattekas et-ha-makhaneh u-va-boqer hayetah shikhvat ha-tal saviv la-makhaneh*. The noun *selav* ("quail," Coturnix coturnix) designates a migratory bird that passes through the Sinai in enormous flocks, arriving exhausted and landing in great numbers. The quail episode is brief here -- a single verse -- compared to the extended account in Numbers 11:31-34. The verb *vattekas* ("and it covered," from *kasah*) uses the same verb used for the Sea covering the Egyptians (14:28; 15:5, 10) -- a verbal echo linking divine provision to divine judgment. The *shikhvat ha-tal* ("layer of dew," from *shakhav*, "to lie down") describes the moisture beneath which the manna appears (cf. Num. 11:9). All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *ortugomētra* ("quail-mother") for *selav*.

> **[fn13] v.14 -- *Daq mekhuspas*: "a fine, flaky thing" -- the appearance of manna** -- Hebrew: *vatta'al shikhvat ha-tal ve-hinneh al-penei ha-midbar daq mekhuspas daq ka-kefor al-ha-arets*. The adjective *daq* ("thin, fine, small," from *daqaq*, "to be pulverized") is repeated twice, emphasizing the extreme fineness. The word *mekhuspas* is a hapax legomenon (occurs only here), possibly meaning "flaked, scaled, peeled" from an uncertain root. The simile *ka-kefor* ("like frost") compares the manna to the white crystalline covering of hoarfrost. All three translators agree on rendering *daq* as "fine" and *mekhuspas* as "flaky." The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *lepton hōsei korianon leukon hōsei pagos* ("fine like white coriander, like frost"), conflating elements from vv. 14 and 31.

> **[fn14] v.15 -- *Man hu*: "What is it?" -- the folk etymology of manna** -- Hebrew: *vayir'u venei-Yisra'el vayomeru ish el-akhiv man hu ki lo yade'u mah-hu vayomer Mosheh aleihem hu ha-lechem asher natan YHWH lakhem le-okhlah*. The expression *man hu* is the key phrase of the chapter. The word *man* is most likely an archaic or dialectal Hebrew interrogative meaning "what?" (related to *mah*), yielding "What is it?" The name *man* (manna) derives from this question of bewilderment: the food is so unprecedented it has no name, only a question. The explanatory clause *ki lo yade'u mah-hu* ("for they did not know what it was") confirms the interrogative sense, with standard Hebrew *mah* replacing the dialectal *man*. EMET renders "Man hu [What is it?]" with transliteration and bracketed translation. LOGOS renders "What is it? [man hu]" with bracketed transliteration. PNEUMA renders "*Man hu?* -- 'What is it?'" with italicized transliteration and quoted translation. The consensus renders "*Man hu!*" -- "What is it?" -- preserving both the Hebrew expression (italicized) and the English translation, following PNEUMA's approach of giving the Hebrew first. The Hebrew must remain audible to preserve the folk etymology: the food is called *man* because the people cried *man hu*. LXX: *ti estin touto* ("what is this?") -- translating rather than transliterating, making the etymology accessible to Greek readers but losing the dialectal flavor.

> **[fn15] v.16 -- *Omer la-gulgolet*: "an omer per head" -- the daily measure** -- Hebrew: *zeh ha-davar asher tsivvah YHWH liqtu mimmennu ish lefi okhlo omer la-gulgolet mispar nafshotekhem ish la-asher be-oholo tiqqakhu*. The *omer* is a dry measure defined in v. 36 as one-tenth of an *ephah* (approximately 2.3 liters). The word *la-gulgolet* ("per head," literally "per skull," from *gulgolet*, "skull") is census terminology -- the same word used in Num. 1:2 and the origin of "Golgotha." The phrase *ish lefi okhlo* ("each man according to his eating," literally "a man according to the mouth of his eating") establishes proportional gathering. EMET renders "per head [la-gulgolet]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "per head." PNEUMA renders "per person." The consensus renders "per head" -- following EMET/LOGOS, preserving the idiomatic census language. For *nafshotekhem*, the consensus renders "your persons" (LOGOS) rather than "your souls" -- *nefesh* here means "person, living being" rather than the later theological sense of "soul." LXX: *gomor kata kephalēn* ("a gomor per head").

> **[fn16] v.18 -- *Lo he'edif...lo hekhsir*: "had nothing over...had no lack" -- the miracle of exact sufficiency** -- Hebrew: *vayamoddu va-omer ve-lo he'edif ha-marbeh ve-ha-mam'it lo hekhsir ish lefi-okhlo laqatu*. The Hiphil *he'edif* (from *adaf*, "to be in excess") = "had surplus"; the Hiphil *hekhsir* (from *khasar*, "to lack") = "had deficit." The miracle is remarkable: regardless of how much each person gathered, when measured, everyone had exactly one omer. The manna defies the economics of accumulation and enforces equality. Paul cites this verse in 2 Corinthians 8:15. EMET renders "did not have excess [lo he'edif]...did not lack [lo hakhsir]." LOGOS renders "had nothing over...had no lack." PNEUMA renders "did not have too much...did not have too little." The consensus renders "had nothing over...had no lack" -- following LOGOS for concision and formal elegance. All three agree on the substance. LXX: *ouk epleonasen...ouk ēlattonēsen* ("did not have more...did not have less").

> **[fn17] v.19 -- *Al-yoter mimmennu*: "Let no man leave any of it" -- the prohibition of hoarding** -- Hebrew: *vayomer Mosheh aleihem ish al-yoter mimmennu ad-boqer*. The Hiphil jussive with negative *al-yoter* (from *yatar*, "to remain over") prohibits hoarding. The manna must be consumed the same day -- daily bread, not a commodity to be stored. This enforces radical daily dependence on YHWH. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *mēdeis katalipetō* ("let no one leave behind").

> **[fn18] v.20 -- *Vayarum tola'im vayiv'ash*: "it bred worms and stank" -- the consequence of disobedience** -- Hebrew: *ve-lo-shame'u el-Mosheh vayotiru anashim mimmennu ad-boqer vayarum tola'im vayiv'ash vayiqtsof aleihem Mosheh*. The hoarded manna breeds *tola'im* ("worms, maggots") and becomes foul (*ba'ash*, "to stink"). Moses' anger (*vayiqtsof*, from *qatsaf*) reflects YHWH's displeasure. The divine provision cannot be stockpiled -- it spoils when hoarded. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *exezesen skōlēkas kai epōzesen* ("it bred worms and stank").

> **[fn19] v.21 -- *Ba-boqer ba-boqer*: "morning by morning" -- the daily rhythm** -- Hebrew: *vayilqetu oto ba-boqer ba-boqer ish lefi okhlo ve-kham ha-shemesh ve-namas*. The repetition *ba-boqer ba-boqer* ("in the morning, in the morning") indicates daily regularity. The manna melts (*namas*, Niphal of *masas*, "to melt") when the sun grows hot -- it must be gathered early. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *prōi prōi* ("morning by morning").

> **[fn20] v.22 -- *Nesi'ei ha-edah*: "the leaders of the congregation" -- the sixth-day surprise** -- Hebrew: *vayehi ba-yom ha-shishi laqetu lechem mishneh shenei ha-omer la-ekhad vayavo'u kol-nesi'ei ha-edah vayaggidu le-Mosheh*. The *nesi'im* ("leaders, princes, chiefs," from *nasa*, "to lift up") are surprised by the double quantity and report it to Moses. The double portion confirms YHWH's promise from v. 5. EMET renders "leaders [nesi'ei]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "leaders." PNEUMA renders "leaders." All three agree. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *hoi archontes tēs sunagōgēs* ("the rulers of the synagogue").

> **[fn21] v.23 -- *Shabbaton shabbat-qodesh laYHWH*: "A solemn rest, a holy Sabbath to YHWH" -- the first Sabbath legislation in the Torah** -- Hebrew: *vayomer aleihem hu asher dibber YHWH shabbaton shabbat-qodesh la-YHWH makhar et asher-tofu efu ve-et asher-tevashelu bashelu ve-et kol-ha-odef hannikhu lakhem le-mishmeret ad-ha-boqer*. This is the first explicit Sabbath legislation in the Torah -- preceding Sinai and the Decalogue by four chapters. The noun *shabbaton* (an intensive form from *sh-b-t*, "to cease") denotes complete cessation, emphatic rest. Paired with *shabbat-qodesh* ("a Sabbath of holiness"), it creates a double emphasis: rest-of-rest, holy cessation. The phrase *la-YHWH* dedicates the Sabbath -- the rest belongs to God. The practical instructions (*efu*, "bake," from *afah*; *bashelu*, "boil," from *bashal*) indicate that all cooking must be done on the sixth day. The surplus (*ha-odef*, from *adaf*) is "set aside" (*hannikhu*, Hiphil of *nuakh*, "to rest") for the next day -- on this day alone, the leftover manna will not spoil (v. 24). EMET renders "A Shabbaton [solemn rest] -- a Shabbat [sabbath] of qodesh [holiness]" with extensive transliteration. LOGOS renders "A rest, a holy Sabbath to the LORD." PNEUMA renders "a day of complete rest, a holy Sabbath dedicated to YHWH." The consensus renders "A solemn rest, a holy Sabbath to YHWH" -- combining EMET's "solemn rest" for *shabbaton* (capturing the intensive force that LOGOS's bare "rest" misses) with LOGOS's formal structure. PNEUMA's addition of "day of" and "dedicated" are not in the Hebrew. LXX: *sabbata anapausis hagia tō kuriō* ("Sabbaths, a holy rest to the Lord"), using the plural *sabbata*.

> **[fn22] v.24 -- The Sabbath miracle: manna preserved** -- Hebrew: *vayyannikhu oto ad-ha-boqer ka-asher tsivvah Mosheh ve-lo hiv'ish ve-rimmah lo-hayetah bo*. The manna that is "kept" on the sixth evening does not rot, unlike manna kept on other nights (v. 20). The double negative (*ve-lo hiv'ish*, "it did not stink" + *ve-rimmah lo-hayetah bo*, "and no worm was in it") emphasizes complete preservation. The word *rimmah* ("worm, maggot") is a synonym of *tola'im* (v. 20). The Sabbath transforms the nature of the food. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *ouk epōzesen, oude skōlēx egeneto en autō* ("it did not stink, nor did a worm come to be in it").

> **[fn23] v.25 -- *Ha-yom...ha-yom...ha-yom*: the threefold "today"** -- Hebrew: *vayomer Mosheh ikhluhu ha-yom ki-Shabbat ha-yom la-YHWH ha-yom lo timtsa'uhu ba-sadeh*. The threefold repetition of *ha-yom* ("today") hammers the point: eat it today / a Sabbath today / today you will not find it. Sabbath is present-tense rest. All three translators preserve the triple "today." The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *phagete sēmeron* ("eat today").

> **[fn24] v.27 -- Sabbath disobedience** -- Hebrew: *vayehi ba-yom ha-shevi'i yatse'u min-ha-am lilqot ve-lo matsa'u*. Despite explicit instruction (vv. 25-26), some went out to gather and found nothing. The manna's absence on the Sabbath is itself a divine teaching tool. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS. LXX: *exēlthosan tines ek tou laou sullexai kai ouch heuron* ("some of the people went out to gather and did not find").

> **[fn25] v.28 -- *Torotai*: "My instructions" -- the plural of *torah*** -- Hebrew: *vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh ad-anah me'antem lishmor mitsvotai ve-torotai*. The expression *ad-anah* ("how long? until when?") conveys divine exasperation. The plural *torotai* ("My instructions") is significant: YHWH speaks of multiple instructions -- the manna regulations are *torah*, divine teaching embedded in daily life, before Sinai formalizes the term. EMET renders "my torot [laws/instructions]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "My instructions." PNEUMA renders "my instructions." The consensus renders "My instructions" -- following LOGOS/PNEUMA. LXX: *ton nomon mou* ("my law," singular).

> **[fn26] v.29 -- *Re'u ki-YHWH natan lakhem ha-Shabbat*: "See that YHWH has given you the Sabbath" -- the Sabbath as divine gift** -- Hebrew: *re'u ki-YHWH natan lakhem ha-Shabbat al-ken hu noten lakhem ba-yom ha-shishi lechem yomayim shevu ish takhtav al-yetse ish mi-meqomo ba-yom ha-shevi'i*. The imperative *re'u* ("see!") commands perception. The verb *natan* ("has given") presents the Sabbath as a gift, not a burden. The double provision on the sixth day (*lechem yomayim*, "bread of two days") is the evidence of the gift. The command *shevu* (imperative of *yashav*, "to sit, dwell, remain") with *ish takhtav* ("each man in his place," literally "each man under himself") and the prohibition *al-yetse ish mi-meqomo* ("let no man go out from his place") establish the principle of Sabbath rest as remaining in place -- the basis for the rabbinic concept of *techum Shabbat* ("Sabbath boundary"). EMET renders "See [re'u] that YHWH has given [natan] to you the Shabbat." LOGOS renders "See, the LORD has given you the Sabbath." PNEUMA renders "Understand this: YHWH has given you the Sabbath." The consensus renders "See that YHWH has given you the Sabbath" -- following EMET/LOGOS for the imperative "See" (preserving *re'u* rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "Understand this"). The "that" clause follows EMET's rendering of the Hebrew *ki* as a conjunction introducing the content of perception. LXX: *idete, ho gar kurios edōken humin tēn hēmeran tautēn tōn sabbatōn* ("see, for the Lord has given you this day of the Sabbaths"), adding *hēmeran tautēn* ("this day").

> **[fn27] v.30 -- *Vayishbetu*: "the people rested" -- Israel mirrors the Creator** -- Hebrew: *vayishbetu ha-am ba-yom ha-shevi'i*. The Qal of *shavat* ("to cease, rest") is the verbal root of *Shabbat*. The people finally obey: they *shavat* -- they cease. The same verb is used of God in Genesis 2:2 (*vayishbot*, "and He ceased/rested"). Israel's Sabbath mirrors the Creator's rest. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS, rendering "rested." LXX: *esabbatisen ho laos* ("the people kept sabbath"), coining the verb *sabbatizō* from the Hebrew.

> **[fn28] v.31 -- *Ke-zera gad lavan...ke-tsappichit bi-dvash*: "like the seed of coriander, white...like a wafer with honey" -- the manna described** -- Hebrew: *vayiqre'u veit-Yisra'el et-shemo man ve-hu ke-zera gad lavan ve-ta'mo ke-tsappichit bi-dvash*. The name *man* is now formalized as the official designation. The description provides two characteristics: appearance -- *ke-zera gad lavan* ("like the seed of coriander, white"); coriander (*Coriandrum sativum*) has small, round, whitish seeds. And taste -- *ke-tsappichit bi-dvash* ("like a wafer with honey"); *tsappichit* (from *tsapakh*, possibly "to flatten") is a thin flat cake, occurring only here and in Num. 11:8. The *devash* ("honey") may be bee-honey or date-syrup. EMET renders "seed [ke-zera] of coriander [gad]" and "a wafer [ke-tsappikheit] with honey [bi-dvash]." LOGOS renders "like seed of coriander, white" and "like a wafer with honey." PNEUMA renders "like coriander seed" and "like wafers made with honey." The consensus renders "like the seed of coriander, white" (following LOGOS, preserving the Hebrew word order where *lavan* modifies the appearance) and "like a wafer with honey" (following LOGOS -- PNEUMA's "wafers made with" is interpretive expansion). The designation "house of Israel" (*beit Yisra'el*) rather than "sons of Israel" is preserved as it appears in the Hebrew. LXX: *hōsei sperma koriannou leukon...hōs enkris en meliti* ("like white coriander seed...like a cake in honey").

> **[fn29] v.32 -- *He'ekhalti etkhem*: "I fed you" -- divine first-person feeding** -- Hebrew: *vayomer Mosheh zeh ha-davar asher tsivvah YHWH melo ha-omer mimmennu le-mishmeret le-doroteikhem lema'an yir'u et-ha-lechem asher he'ekhalti etkhem ba-midbar be-hotsi'i etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim*. The Hiphil *he'ekhalti* (from *akhal*, "to eat") = "I caused to eat, I fed." YHWH speaks in the first person: "I fed you." The memorial jar preserves physical evidence for later generations (*le-doroteikhem*) of how YHWH sustained Israel. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus renders "with which I fed you" rather than LOGOS's "which I fed you" -- the preposition clarifies the English syntax. LXX: *hon ephagete humeis* ("which you ate") -- shifting from first-person divine speech to second-person narrative, a notable theological softening.

> **[fn30] v.33 -- *Tsintseneth*: "a jar" -- the hapax legomenon and the LXX's golden jar** -- Hebrew: *vayomer Mosheh el-Aharon qakh tsintseneth akhat ve-ten-shammah melo-ha-omer man ve-hannakh oto lifnei YHWH le-mishmeret le-doroteikhem*. The noun *tsintseneth* is a hapax legomenon -- it occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. Its etymology is uncertain; it may relate to an Egyptian root for a container. The Hebrew text does not specify the material. The LXX, however, renders *stamnon chrusoun* ("a golden jar"), adding the adjective "golden" -- a detail absent from the MT but reflected in Hebrews 9:4 (*chrusēn stamnon echousan to manna*, "a golden jar having the manna"). Whether the LXX reflects an older tradition or represents an interpretive expansion is debated. The consensus follows the MT and renders simply "a jar," preserving the Hebrew's lack of material specification. The LXX divergence is noted but not adopted. EMET renders "tsintseneth [jar]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "jar." PNEUMA renders "a jar." All three follow the MT. LXX: *stamnon chrusoun* ("a golden jar").

> **[fn31] v.34 -- *Lifnei ha-Edut*: "before the Testimony" -- the proleptic reference** -- Hebrew: *ka-asher tsivvah YHWH el-Mosheh vayyannikhehu Aharon lifnei ha-Edut le-mishmaret*. The noun *Edut* ("Testimony, witness," from *ud*, "to testify") refers to the stone tablets of the covenant and by extension to the Ark of the Testimony in which they are placed. This is a proleptic (anticipatory) reference: the Ark and Tabernacle have not yet been built at this point in the narrative (they are constructed in chs. 25-40). The manna jar placed "before the Testimony" creates a memorial link between daily provision and covenant law: the bread from heaven rests beside the words from heaven. EMET renders "the edut [testimony]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "the Testimony." PNEUMA renders "the Testimony." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "the Testimony" with capital T, preserving the technical covenantal sense. LXX: *enantion tou theou* ("before God"), avoiding the specific reference to the Ark.

> **[fn32] v.35 -- *Arba'im shanah*: "forty years" -- the full duration** -- Hebrew: *u-venei Yisra'el akhlu et-ha-man arba'im shanah ad-bo'am el-erets noshavet et-ha-man akhlu ad-bo'am el-qetseh erets Kena'an*. The repetition (*et-ha-man akhlu...et-ha-man akhlu*) emphasizes completeness, with two endpoints: *erets noshavet* ("an inhabited land" -- any settled land) and *qetseh erets Kena'an* ("the border of the land of Canaan" -- the specific destination). The manna ceases when they reach productive land (cf. Josh. 5:12). This verse is an editorial note written from the perspective of settlement. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS, rendering "an inhabited land" for *erets noshavet* (Niphal participle of *yashav*, "to dwell"). LXX: *eis gēn oikoumenēn* ("to inhabited land"); *eis meros tēs Phoinikēs* ("to a part of Phoenicia") for *erets Kena'an*.

> **[fn33] v.36 -- *Ve-ha-omer asirit ha-efah hu*: "the omer is a tenth of the ephah" -- the editorial gloss** -- Hebrew: *ve-ha-omer asirit ha-efah hu*. This is a parenthetical editorial note explaining the *omer* measure for readers unfamiliar with it. The *ephah* (*efah*) was the standard dry measure, approximately 22 liters. A tenth of the *ephah* -- the *omer* -- is approximately 2.2 liters. The *omer* as a unit of measure appears almost exclusively in Exodus 16; elsewhere *omer* refers to a sheaf of grain (Lev. 23:10-15). The editorial nature of this note suggests it was added for an audience for whom the *omer* is no longer a commonly understood unit. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS, rendering the verse in parentheses to mark its parenthetical, editorial character. LXX: *to de gomor to dekaton tōn triōn metrōn ēn* ("the gomor was a tenth of the three measures"), interpreting the *ephah* as three seahs.

---
